title: Near-Linear Time Approximation Algorithm for Balanced Separator
speaker: Sushant Sachdeva
abstract: The goal of the Balanced Separator problem is to find a balanced cut in a given graph G(V,E), while minimizing the number of edges that cross the cut. It is a fundamental problem with applications in clustering, image segmentation, community detection, and as a primitive for divide-and-conquer on graphs.

proof_stages.label: Balanced separator target and spectral benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates near-linear graph algorithms, defines conductance and balanced separator, recalls Cheeger-style spectral approximation and prior flow/spectral algorithms, and states the goal of an m polylog n-time spectral approximation without polynomial dependence on 1/gamma.

proof_stages.label: Random-walk-to-cut reduction framework
proof_stages.summary: Random-walk mixing is related to graph cuts, a generic simulate-round-modify separator framework is described, heat-kernel walks are defined, and the main reduction to simulating O(log^2 n) continuous-time random walks at time about log n/gamma is stated.

proof_stages.label: Heat-kernel separator iteration and potential drop
proof_stages.summary: The separator algorithm embeds vertices by their heat-kernel distributions, uses an L2 potential measuring distance from stationarity, rounds long vectors to either find a balanced cut or identify unbalanced low-conductance ball cuts, and adds structured star edges so the potential drops by a constant factor over O(log n) rounds.

proof_stages.label: Simulation objective and polynomial barrier
proof_stages.summary: After reducing the graph problem to computing exp(-tau L)v, the talk explains why exact diagonalization and Taylor expansion are too slow, obtains a sqrt(tau)-degree polynomial approximation, and proves via Markov-type lower bounds that polynomial methods cannot remove this tau dependence.

proof_stages.label: Rational approximation and SDD inverses
proof_stages.summary: The Saff-Schonhage-Varga rational approximation expresses the exponential through a logarithmic-degree polynomial in (I+tau L/k)^(-1), so heat-kernel simulation is reduced to a small number of SDD linear-system solves that can be performed in near-linear time.

proof_stages.label: Krylov/Lanczos implementation of the existential approximation
proof_stages.summary: Lanczos is used on B=(I+tau L/k)^(-1) to build a small Krylov subspace, restrict B to a k-by-k matrix T, and compute V f(T) e_1 so that the returned vector is as accurate as the best degree-k polynomial approximation without needing explicit coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Robust approximate solves and final assembly
proof_stages.summary: The analysis is made robust to inexact Spielman-Teng solves and symmetrization errors, the SDD/structured-star updates are handled, and the fast heat-kernel simulator is combined with the separator iteration to yield the near-linear-time spectral balanced-separator algorithm and its open directions.

examples.name: Tiny dangling low-conductance cut
examples.note: A four-vertex set with one outgoing edge illustrates why a Cheeger cut may have low conductance yet fail to be balanced, motivating recursive or soft removal of small cuts.
examples.engagement_reason: the four-vertex dangling cut with one outgoing edge is used only to illustrate an unbalanced low-conductance obstruction, with no concrete conductance calculation or worked instance
